sourceMovie.title: Blade Runner
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Blade Runner works as a science fiction movie with a point of view—fans quote moments, but they also argue about what the film is actually saying about power, desire, or survival. The science fiction and drama blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Blade Runner 2049
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind Blade Runner 2049 (2017) clearly understood what makes science-fiction work — the same quality that keeps Blade Runner rewatchable. Expect the same tense rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

recommendations.title: The Terminator
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Terminator (1984) is the kind of film Blade Runner fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The science-fiction overlap is real, but it's the tense emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

recommendations.title: Insurgent
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Insurgent shares Blade Runner's gift for science-fiction — the 2015 film delivers thriller without overstaying its welcome. Consider it required viewing once you've finished rewatching your first pick.

recommendations.title: Cloud Atlas
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Much of what makes Blade Runner compelling — the science-fiction, the drama — shows up in Cloud Atlas (2012) in a slightly different form. Both films understand that tense storytelling works best when it trusts the audience.

recommendations.title: Upgrade
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The science-fiction energy of Blade Runner is alive in Upgrade (2018), filtered through a different story but the same emotional register. It earns its place on this list with a tense payoff that rivals the original pick.

recommendations.title: A Scanner Darkly
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: A Scanner Darkly (2006) operates in the same science-fiction as Blade Runner, but with its own distinct voice and pacing. The uplifting payoff will feel satisfyingly familiar without retreading the same ground.

recommendations.title: Murder on the Orient Express
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Murder on the Orient Express (1974) belongs to the same conversation as Blade Runner — both anchor their drama in thriller emotion that doesn't let go. The tense undercurrent connects them more than any plot summary would suggest.

recommendations.title: The Thirteenth Floor
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Blade Runner's science-fiction stayed with you, The Thirteenth Floor (1999) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The thriller through-line is the real common thread between them.

recommendations.title: Black Rain
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where Blade Runner excels in drama, Black Rain (1989) doubles down on thriller to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

recommendations.title: Subservience
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Subservience (2024) is the natural next step after Blade Runner: same science-fiction DNA, different perspective. The dark streak and thriller elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

faq.question: What should I watch after Blade Runner?
faq.answer: Try Blade Runner 2049, The Terminator, Insurgent—each shares DNA with Blade Runner while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Blade Runner?
faq.answer: Blade Runner (1982) is best described as Science Fiction, Drama, Thriller. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Blade Runner part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Blade Runner Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
